factors.summary: Regulatory changes are creating opportunities and challenges, particularly in areas like environmental compliance, which is driving demand for certain products.

evidence.text: The increased fourth quarter demand for retail refrigeration products is expected to continue, as the recent wear and tear on our customers' existing assets will drive equipment replacement programs. [...] The organic revenue decrease for the year ended December 31, 2019 was driven [...] partially offset by increased project activity for can-shaping equipment.

factors.summary: Environmental regulations are creating opportunities in areas like can-shaping equipment, driven by a shift from plastic to aluminum containers.

evidence.text: We expect continued strength in the aluminum can-shaping market driven by [...] a secular shift with beverage companies switching from plastic to aluminum containers for environmental and other reasons.

factors.summary: Trade tariffs are impacting costs, particularly in areas with U.S. Section 232 and 301 tariff exposure, leading to increased material and inflation costs.

evidence.text: increased material costs due, in part, to U.S. Section 232 and 301 tariff exposure, inflation costs, and unfavorable product and regional mix.

factors.summary: The company's capital expenditure outlook is mixed. While there was a decrease in 2020 compared to 2019 due to COVID-19 impacts, there are efforts to manage CapEx cautiously to support growth and efficiency.

evidence.text: Our capital expenditures decreased $21.1 million in 2020 compared to 2019 in line with our reduced capital spending plan for the year as a result of COVID-19, without deferring strategic ongoing initiatives.
We also reduced our capital spending for the year, without deferring strategic ongoing initiatives.
The company is managing capital expenditures to align with demand and efficiency improvements, as indicated by the focus on operational optimization and IT centralization.
evidence.subsection: Rightsizing Activities, which includes Restructuring and Other Costs

evidence.text: The company remains in a strong financial position, with sufficient liquidity available for reinvestment in existing businesses and strategic acquisitions.

factors.summary: The company consistently prioritizes shareholder returns through regular dividend payments and share repurchases, demonstrating confidence in its financial health. The stability in dividend payments and share buyback activities over the years reflects a balanced approach to managing shareholder returns.

evidence.text: Total dividend payments to common shareholders were $284.3 million in 2020, $282.2 million in 2019 and $283.6 million in 2018.
We also continued our 65 year history of increasing our annual dividend per share and paid a total of $284.3 million in dividends to our shareholders.
Diluted earnings per share also improved due to the benefit of the prior and current year share repurchases.
The company remains in a strong financial position, with sufficient liquidity available for reinvestment in existing businesses and strategic acquisitions.

factors.summary: The company has been making progress in managing its debt leverage, as evidenced by the decrease in the net debt to net capitalization ratio and refinancing efforts at lower interest rates. However, some challenges remain from prior debt levels, though the overall financial position is strong with sufficient liquidity.

evidence.text: Our net debt to net capitalization ratio decreased to 43.4% at December 31, 2020 compared to 46.8% at December 31, 2019.
Interest expense, net of interest income, decreased $12.9 million, or 10.7%, to $108.4 million compared with 2019 primarily due to lower interest rates on new debt issued in November 2019 [...].
We remain in a strong financial position, with sufficient liquidity available for reinvestment in existing businesses and strategic acquisitions.

factors.summary: Free cash flow improved in 2020 compared to 2019, driven by higher operating cash flows, cost containment, and operational efficiency. While some segments experienced cash flow declines due to reduced revenues, overall free cash flow generation showed positive trends with significant contributions from improved working capital, deferred tax payments, and reduced capital expenditures.

evidence.text: For 2020, we generated free cash flow of $939.1 million, representing 14.1% of revenue and 137.4% of earnings from continuing operations. The full year increase in 2020 free cash flow reflects higher cash flow provided by operations primarily due to improvements in working capital, permitted deferrals of tax payments and advanced payments received on contracts, as well as lower capital expenditures.
Cash Flows from Continuing Operations: Net cash flows provided by (used in): Operating activities $ 1,104,810 [...] Investing activities (481,379) [...] Financing activities (506,290)
evidence.subsection: Introduction / ITEM 7. MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS

evidence.text: These initiatives include actions to adjust direct and indirect manufacturing costs to current demand levels, the execution of short-term actions to reduce labor costs, the elimination of non-essential travel, third party and other expenses, the recognition of adjustments to variable compensation to reflect current conditions, the execution of selective structural cost actions aimed at streamlining our businesses, and a detailed review and re-prioritization of all planned investments and hiring plans.
evidence.subsection: Rightsizing Activities, which includes Restructuring and Other Costs

factors.summary: The company's margins are showing mixed performance across different segments. While gross profit margins are expanding due to productivity and restructuring actions, other segments like Engineered Products and Refrigeration & Food Equipment are experiencing margin compression, primarily due to reduced demand and operational inefficiencies related to the COVID-19 pandemic.

evidence.text: Gross profit margin expanded 30 basis points to 37.0% as compared to the prior year due to benefits from productivity initiatives and restructuring and cost containment actions.

Segment margin decreased from 17.2% to 15.6% as compared to the prior year. This decrease was primarily driven by the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on customer spending, partially offset by cost containment initiatives we executed in the year.

Segment margin increased to 23.1% from 18.6% as compared to the prior year. [...] Segment margin increased to 17.9% from 17.8% in the prior year, an increase of 10 basis points. [...] Segment margin decreased to 7.8% from 8.5% in the prior year due to substantially reduced revenues and operational inefficiencies associated with COVID-19, increased restructuring expenses and a $3.6 million write-off of assets.

factors.summary: The company is actively managing costs through various programs, including rightsizing, restructuring, and operational efficiency initiatives, which have resulted in measurable savings. However, there is some indication of ongoing challenges, particularly in adjusting to lower demand and implementing structural changes, as evidenced by restructuring charges and rightsizing costs.

evidence.text: Cost reduction programs are delivering measurable savings, with restructuring charges supporting these actions.

During the year ended December 31, 2020, we executed rightsizing programs to further optimize operations. Rightsizing charges of $51.5 million included restructuring charges of $44.5 million and other costs of $7.0 million [...].

Restructuring expense was comprised primarily of new actions executed in response to lower demand driven by COVID-19 as well as continuing broad-based selling, general and administrative expense reduction initiatives and broad-based operational efficiency initiatives focusing on footprint consolidation, and operational optimization and IT centralization.
evidence.subsection: Rightsizing Activities, which includes Restructuring and Other Costs

evidence.text: These initiatives included actions to adjust direct and indirect manufacturing cost to current demand levels, the execution of short-term actions to reduce labor costs, the elimination of non-essential travel, third party and other expenses, [...] and a detailed review and re-prioritization of all planned investments and hiring plans. [...] Segment earnings increased significantly excluding the loss on sale of Finder driven by volume leverage, pricing initiatives, and productivity actions, as well as reduced rightsizing costs.

factors.summary: The company is experiencing rising input costs, primarily driven by increases in commodity prices, tariffs, and inflation. While the company is implementing productivity initiatives and cost containment measures to mitigate these challenges, it remains exposed to the risks of delayed price adjustments and potential competitor pricing pressures. Additionally, supply chain disruptions and global impacts like the COVID-19 pandemic further complicate cost management efforts.

evidence.text: Some of our businesses are exposed to volatility in the prices of certain commodities, such as aluminum, steel, copper and various precious metals, among others. Our primary exposure to commodity pricing volatility relates to the use of these materials in purchased component parts or the purchase of raw materials.

Significant price increases for certain commodities, other raw materials or components could adversely affect operating profits of our businesses. While we generally attempt to mitigate the impact of increased raw material prices by hedging or passing along the increased costs to customers, there may be a time delay between the increased raw material prices and the ability to increase the prices of products, or we may be unable to increase the prices of products due to a competitor’s pricing pressure or other factors.

Gross profit decreased due to lower revenue as productivity initiatives including prior rightsizing programs and cost containment actions were partially offset by increased material costs and inflation and higher restructuring costs.

Increases in material costs due, in part, to U.S. Section 232 and 301 tariff exposure, as well as a loss due to the after-tax extinguishment of debt of $18.4 million and a loss on assets held for sale of $46.9 million.

These benefits were partially offset by the global impact of COVID-19 on volume and an unfavorable impact from material and inflation costs. [...] These benefits were partially offset by increased material costs due, in part, to U.S. Section 232 and 301 tariff exposure, inflation costs, and unfavorable product and regional mix.

factors.summary: The company faces significant geopolitical risks, particularly from trade tensions and political instability in various regions. These risks impact its supply chain, operations, and costs, especially due to tariffs and inflation. While foreign currency derivatives are used to mitigate some risks, the overall exposure remains substantial.

evidence.text: We conduct business in various non-U.S. countries, including Canada, substantially all of the European countries, Mexico, Brazil, China, India and other Asian countries. Therefore, we have foreign currency risk relating to receipts from customers, payments to suppliers and intercompany transactions denominated in foreign currencies. We will occasionally use derivative financial instruments to offset such risks, when it is believed that the exposure will not be limited by our normal operating and financing activities.

Our businesses are subject to regulation and their profitability and reputation could be adversely affected by domestic and foreign governmental and public policy changes, risks associated with emerging markets, changes in statutory tax rates and unanticipated outcomes with respect to tax audits.

Increases in material costs due, in part, to U.S. Section 232 and 301 tariff exposure, as well as a loss due to the after-tax extinguishment of debt of $18.4 million and a loss on assets held for sale of $46.9 million.
increased material costs due, in part, to U.S. Section 232 and 301 tariff exposure, inflation costs, and unfavorable product and regional mix.

factors.summary: The company faces potential litigation risks that could impact its financial health, particularly from product liability and other legal proceedings. However, there is no mention of material pending cases in the 2020 results, indicating that litigation exposure is not currently a significant factor.

evidence.text: The company is exposed to potential litigation risks that could impact its financial health.
evidence.subsection: • Unforeseen developments in contingencies such as litigation and product recalls could adversely affect our consolidated results of operations, financial condition and cash flows.

evidence.text: We and certain of our subsidiaries are, and from time to time may become, parties to a number of legal proceedings incidental to our businesses, including alleged injuries arising out of the use of products or exposure to hazardous substances, or claims related to patent infringement, employment matters and commercial disputes. The defense of these lawsuits may require significant expenses and divert management’s attention, and we may be required to pay damages that could adversely affect our consolidated results of operations, financial condition and cash flows.
evidence.subsection: • Our growth and results of operations may be adversely affected if we are unsuccessful in our capital allocation and acquisition program.

evidence.text: The company remains in a strong financial position, with sufficient liquidity available for reinvestment in existing businesses and strategic acquisitions.

factors.summary: The company's interest rate exposure is mixed, with minimal impact on margins due to long-term fixed-rate debt but benefiting from lower borrowing costs. While the structure protects against margin fluctuations, the decrease in interest expense highlights tangible benefits from rate changes.

evidence.text: We consider our current risk related to market fluctuations in interest rates to be minimal since our debt is largely long-term and fixed in nature. Generally, the fair market value of fixed-interest rate debt will increase as interest rates fall and decrease as interest rates rise. A 100 basis point increase in market interest rates would decrease the 2020 year-end fair value of our long-term debt by approximately $280.7 million. However, since we have no plans to repurchase our outstanding fixed-rate instruments before their maturities, the impact of market interest rate fluctuations on our long-term debt does not affect our results of operations or financial position.
Interest expense, net of interest income, decreased $12.9 million, or 10.7%, to $108.4 million compared with 2019 primarily due to lower interest rates on new debt issued in November 2019 [...].

factors.summary: The company faces mixed impacts from foreign exchange movements, with some areas experiencing negative effects and others showing positive outcomes. While revenue is not significantly affected, foreign exchange can influence costs and margins, and the company is managing this exposure through hedging and operational adjustments.

evidence.text: Fluctuations in foreign exchange rates could negatively impact the company's reported results and export competitiveness.
Cross-border transactions, both with external parties and intercompany relationships, result in increased exposure to foreign exchange effects.
Foreign currency translation had no significant impact to revenue for the year ended December 31, 2020.

Unfavorable impact from foreign currency translation of 1.0% [...] favorable impact from foreign currency translation of 0.6%. [...] unfavorable impact from foreign currency translation of 1.2%

factors.summary: The company faces potential risks related to labor disputes, including work stoppages and union campaigns, which could negatively impact productivity and financial results. However, the management's discussion and analysis section does not mention any current labor issues or strikes, indicating relatively stable labor relations at present.

evidence.text: We have a number of collective bargaining units in the United States and various foreign collective labor arrangements. We are subject to potential work stoppages, union and works council campaigns and other labor disputes, any of which could adversely impact our productivity, reputation, consolidated results of operations, financial condition and cash flows.
evidence.subsection: • If we experience work stoppages, union and works council campaigns and other labor disputes, our productivity and results of operations could be adversely impacted.

factors.summary: The supply chain is showing mixed signs of improvement and ongoing challenges. While certain regions and specific businesses are experiencing improvements post-COVID disruptions, there are still lingering issues due to reliance on single-source suppliers, geopolitical factors, and the fluid nature of the public health situation.

evidence.text: The public health situation, global response measures and corresponding impacts on various markets remain fluid and uncertain and may lead to sudden changes in trajectory and outlook.

Some of the raw materials and components may be available only from limited or single source suppliers. If a single source or limited source supplier were to cease or interrupt production for any reason or otherwise fail to supply those raw materials or components to us on favorable purchase terms, including at favorable prices, in sufficient quantities and with adequate lead times needed for efficient manufacturing, our ability to meet customer commitments, and satisfy market demands for affected products could be negatively affected.

Activity improved sequentially from the second quarter into the second half of the year in a number of businesses, most notably in our vehicle service, industrial automation, and fluid dispensing businesses, as government mandated restrictions eased and customer demand accelerated.
The supply chain is improving, though there are still disruptions from the pandemic and geopolitical factors affecting certain areas.

factors.summary: The company is actively pursuing a robust M&A strategy, which has successfully created value through strategic acquisitions. These acquisitions have enhanced capabilities, expanded market presence, and contributed to growth in both revenue and backlog. However, the company faces risks, including challenges in finding suitable acquisition targets and successfully integrating them.

evidence.text: During the year ended December 31, 2020, we made a total of six acquisitions totaling $335.8 million, net of cash acquired.
evidence.subsection: • Our growth and results of operations may be adversely affected if we are unsuccessful in our capital allocation and acquisition program.

evidence.text: Acquisition-related growth was driven by the acquisition of Soft-Pak and Belanger.
Acquisition-related growth was driven by the acquisition of Systech in the first quarter and Solaris, which closed in the third quarter.

We expect to continue our strategy of seeking to acquire value creating add-on businesses that broaden our existing position and global reach as well as, in the right circumstances, strategically pursue larger acquisitions that could have the potential to either complement our existing businesses or allow us to pursue a new platform. However, there can be no assurance that we will be able to continue to find suitable businesses to purchase, that we will be able to acquire such businesses on acceptable terms, or that all closing conditions will be satisfied with respect to any pending acquisition.

factors.summary: The company has demonstrated consistent success in maintaining and increasing pricing across its segments, with customer pricing favorably impacting revenue in both 2020 and 2019. The evidence shows a favorable impact of approximately 0.7% in 2020 and between 0.3% to 1.9% in previous years, indicating strong pricing power without significant discounting issues.

evidence.text: Customer pricing favorably impacted revenue by approximately 0.7% for the year [2020].
Customer pricing favorably impacted revenue by approximately 0.3% in 2020 and 1.9% in 2019.
Customer pricing favorably impacted revenue by approximately 0.7% in 2020. [...] Customer pricing favorably impacted revenue by approximately 1.0% in 2019.

factors.summary: The company's backlog orders are growing across all segments, reflecting strong demand and recovery post-COVID-19. Significant increases were observed in Imaging & Identification, Pumps & Process Solutions, and Refrigeration & Food Equipment, driven by key market areas such as biopharma, can-shaping, and heat exchangers.

evidence.text: Backlog as of December 31, 2020 was $1.8 billion, up from $1.5 billion from the prior year. The increase in backlog occurred in the third and fourth quarters of 2020 as order rates sequentially improved after a significant second quarter decline due to COVID-19.
Backlog increased 53.3% compared to the prior year driven by the Systech and Solaris acquisitions and strong order intake in marking and coding.
Backlog increased 10.5% compared to the prior year primarily driven by our biopharma and hygienic markets.
Backlog increased 59.2% over the prior year, principally driven by our aluminum can-shaping equipment business as well as an increase in our retail refrigeration and heat exchanger businesses.

factors.summary: Demand trends overall show signs of improvement post-COVID-19, though recovery varies across different segments and markets. While some areas like vehicle service and industrial automation have seen notable recovery, others such as digital printing and the energy sector remain slow. Deferred orders in certain businesses are beginning to recover, but full recovery is expected to take longer in some markets.

evidence.text: Over the course of 2020, we experienced sequentially improving activity in most markets and geographies, though demand remains lower than historical averages across markets that we expect will take longer to fully recover.

Despite the global economic downturn precipitated by the COVID-19 pandemic, activity improved sequentially from the second quarter into the second half of the year in a number of businesses, most notably in our vehicle service, industrial automation, and fluid dispensing businesses, as government mandated restrictions eased and customer demand accelerated.

Digital textile printing activity has improved since the second quarter of the year especially in sportswear and home decor markets, however demand for capital equipment for apparel printing remains slower compared to the prior year as we exit 2020.

The strong bookings growth in the biopharma and hygienic markets during the fourth quarter of 2020 is expected to continue into 2021 as the underlying robust long-term growth in single-use bioproduction technologies is further supported by demand for COVID-19 related therapies and vaccines.

Many key customers in our retail refrigeration business deferred previously planned projects [...] with a solid recovery in the third and fourth quarters, [...] driven by increased project activity in can-shaping equipment, [...] and strengthening heat exchanger demand across all regions.

factors.summary: The revenue mix is shifting towards higher-margin segments such as marking and coding, biopharma, and can-shaping equipment, which are driving growth. However, there are challenges in lower-margin areas like digital printing and energy, with some businesses experiencing revenue declines due to reduced demand and softer market conditions.

evidence.text: Gross profit margin expanded to 37.0% for the year ended December 31, 2020 compared to 36.7% for the prior year.
Gross profit margin expanded 30 basis points to 37.0% as compared to the prior year due to benefits from productivity initiatives and restructuring and cost containment actions.
Segment margin increased from 15.5% to 17.2% as compared to the prior year.

Our largest business in this segment, marking and coding, posted a modest revenue decline for the year, as we saw solid demand for consumables [...] and strong second half growth in demand for equipment.

factors.summary: Organic revenue growth is decelerating across the company, primarily due to the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic. While some segments like Imaging & Identification experienced a modest decline, others such as Pumps & Process Solutions and Refrigeration & Food Equipment showed signs of recovery towards the end of 2020. Overall, the pandemic has been a significant driver of the deceleration in organic growth.

evidence.text: Organic revenue decline of 6.6% [...] The decline included an organic revenue decline of 6.6% driven by COVID-19 [...].

The strong bookings growth in the biopharma and hygienic markets during the fourth quarter of 2020 is expected to continue into 2021 as the underlying robust long-term growth in single-use bioproduction technologies is further supported by demand for COVID-19 related therapies and vaccines.

factors.summary: The company's key sensitivities include foreign exchange movements, economic conditions influenced by government policies, and commodity prices. The economic environment, whether as a tailwind or headwind, significantly influences business performance.

factors.summary: The company's R&D investment is stable, with consistent costs as a percentage of revenue, and is focused on new product development, innovation, and long-term growth opportunities. Initiatives are on track to support future revenue and margin growth.

evidence.text: Research and development costs [...] amounted to $142.1 million, $141.0 million and $143.0 million for the years ended December 31, 2020, 2019 and 2018, respectively. These costs as a percent of revenue were 2.1%, 2.0% and 2.0% [...].
The company is investing in new product development and innovation, as indicated by the focus on operational efficiency and market expansion.
The company remains in a strong financial position, with sufficient liquidity available for reinvestment in existing businesses and strategic acquisitions.

factors.summary: The company identifies several catalysts for growth, including new product launches, market recoveries, strong demand in key sectors like biopharma and can-shaping, successful integration of acquisitions, and potential cost savings from productivity initiatives. However, these opportunities are tempered by challenges such as the global impact of COVID-19 on volumes and material inflation costs.

factors.summary: The company is actively adopting digital technologies and automation to enhance competitiveness, drive efficiency, and gain strategic advantages, particularly in manufacturing and service areas. These efforts include investments in software solutions, restructuring to reduce costs, and strategic planning for future investments and hiring.

evidence.text: Delivering innovative equipment and components, consumable supplies, aftermarket parts, software and digital solutions and support services.

evidence.text: These initiatives include actions to adjust direct and indirect manufacturing costs to current demand levels, the execution of short-term actions to reduce labor costs, the elimination of non-essential travel, third party and other expenses, the recognition of adjustments to variable compensation to reflect current conditions, the execution of selective structural cost actions aimed at streamlining our businesses, and a detailed review and re-prioritization of all planned investments and hiring plans.

factors.summary: Equipment replacement cycles are mixed across different regions and industries. While some regions, particularly China, are experiencing a deceleration in replacement cycles due to the expiration of government mandates, other areas are seeing acceleration driven by increased demand and wear and tear on existing assets.

evidence.text: The decline was driven mainly by a reduction in China where our Fueling Solutions segment [...] faced significant headwinds due to the expiration of the government's double-wall upgrade mandate [...].

factors.summary: Dealer/distributor inventory levels are healthy, with order rates improving towards the end of 2020. The company is managing these levels through rightsizing activities, which have been reflected in various financial statements. Strong order intake and increased backlog indicate growing end-user demand in key markets, supported by factors such as acquisitions and market growth.

evidence.text: The increase in backlog occurred in the third and fourth quarters of 2020 as order rates sequentially improved after a significant second quarter decline due to COVID-19.
These rightsizing charges were recorded in cost of goods and services, selling, general and administrative expenses, interest expense, and other income, net in the Consolidated Statement of Earnings.

evidence.text: Backlog increased 53.3% compared to the prior year driven by the Systech and Solaris acquisitions and strong order intake in marking and coding.
Backlog increased 10.5% compared to the prior year primarily driven by our biopharma and hygienic markets.
Backlog increased 59.2% over the prior year, principally driven by our aluminum can-shaping equipment business as well as an increase in our retail refrigeration and heat exchanger businesses.

factors.summary: The aftermarket/service revenue is showing mixed performance across different segments. While some areas like Pumps & Process Solutions and industrial automation are experiencing growth, others are facing demand weaknesses, particularly in compression components and certain consumables.

evidence.text: The organic revenue decline was principally driven by continued weakness in demand for compression components and aftermarket services [...].

The organic revenue decline was primarily driven by [...] solid demand for consumables throughout the year, particularly inks for food and beverage, cleaning supplies, pharmaceuticals and medical supplies, and strong second half growth in demand for equipment, as customers resumed equipment purchases in the second half of 2020 as COVID-19 restrictions eased and marking and coding activity remained solid.

factors.summary: Economic conditions present a mixed outlook, with challenges from the lingering impact of COVID-19 leading to disruptions in supply chains and reduced demand across various sectors, though there are signs of recovery in certain areas.

evidence.text: The COVID-19 pandemic disrupted the global economy and adversely impacted our business, including demand for our products across multiple end-markets as well as our supply chain and operations.

Despite a revenue decline of 6.3% due to the impact of COVID-19, earnings increased due to broad-based cost containment actions, benefits from productivity and restructuring actions, a loss due to the after-tax extinguishment of debt of $18.4 million in the prior year and a loss on assets held for sale of $46.9 million.

The global economic downturn precipitated by the COVID-19 pandemic beginning late in the first quarter, which materially impacted our digital textile printing business, as government-mandated mobility restrictions forced clothing and apparel retailers to close locations, and resulted in the decline of overall demand for clothing and apparel, as fewer consumers went to schools, offices, restaurants and other gatherings.
